In [[physics]], '''Planck's constant''', named after the physicist [[Max Planck]], is a fundamental value equal to:

  h = 6.6261 x 10<sup>-34</sup> Js 

appearing in all the equations of [[quantum mechanics]]. It can be seen as a conversion factor between [[frequency]] and [[energy]], especially for [[photon|photons]]. Sometimes the abbreviation

  h_bar = h / 2π

is used, where π is [[Pi|Archimedes' constant]], the ratio of the circumference of a circle to its diameter. 

see [[Electromagnetic radiation]], [[Schrodinger wave equation|Schrödinger's equation]], [[Uncertainty Principle|Heisenberg's uncertainty principle]], [[Wave-Particle duality]]

In [[physics]], '''Planck's constant''', named after the physicist [[Max Planck]], is a fundamental value equal to:



:''h'' = 6.6261 × 10<sup>-34</sup> Js 



appearing in all the equations of [[quantum mechanics]]. It can be seen as a conversion factor between [[frequency]] and [[energy]], especially for [[photon|photons]]. The [[unicode]] symbol ℎ represents Planck's constant. Sometimes the abbreviation



:''h_bar'' = ''h'' / 2π



is used, where π is [[Pi|Archimedes' constant]], the ratio of the circumference of a circle to its diameter. h_bar is a lower-case h with a line through it, the symbol ℏ.



One of the values that can be derived from Planck's constant is the Planck length, [[1e-35 m|1.6 × 10<sup>-35</sup>m]], which is the smallest meaningful length in [[quantum mechanics]]; any two points separated by less than the Planck length are indistinguishable from each other. Similarly, the amount of time it takes a photon to travel one Planck length is Planck time: 10<sup>-43</sup> seconds. This is the smallest meaningful division of time.



The planck length and planck time are used as the fundamental units in the system of [[Planck units]].



:''see also:'' [[Electromagnetic radiation]], [[Schrodinger wave equation|Schrödinger's equation]], [[Uncertainty Principle|Heisenberg's uncertainty principle]], [[Wave-Particle duality]]

In [[physics]], '''Planck's constant''', named after the physicist [[Max Planck]], is a fundamental value approximately equal to:

:''h'' = 6.6261 × 10<sup>-34</sup> Js 

appearing in all the equations of [[quantum mechanics]]. It can be seen as a conversion factor between [[frequency]] and [[energy]], especially for [[photon|photons]]. The [[unicode]] symbol ℎ represents Planck's constant. Sometimes the abbreviation

:''h_bar'' = ''h'' / 2π

is used, where π is [[Pi|Archimedes' constant]], the ratio of the circumference of a circle to its diameter. h_bar is a lower-case h with a line through it, the symbol ℏ.

h-bar (not h) is what occurs in physics as the quantum of spin, in the uncertainty principle, and in the definition of the Planck length. It is possible that h-bar is more fundamental and that h should be defined as an auxilliary equal to 2 pi times h-bar. Certainly when one says "appearing in all the equations of quantum mechanics" what is meant must include h-bar. In a field like quantum electrodynamics (where the normal format for frequency is angular) you forget h ever existed and use h-bar for all purposes.

One of the quantities usually derived from h-bar is the Planck length, [[1e-35 m|1.6 × 10<sup>-35</sup>m]]. Planck defined this in his 1899 paper and gave a value close to the one in use today. His paper says nothing about it's being "the smallest meaningful length in quantum mechanics" although some contemporary physicists talk like that. In 1899 quantum mechanics had not been invented yet. It might or might not be helpful to say "two points separated by less than the Planck length are indistinguishable from each other". This is an issue for today's physicists irrelevant to the original definition of the Planck length a hundred years ago. Similarly, the amount of time it takes a photon to travel one Planck length is Planck time: 0.54 × [[1e-44 s|10<sup>-43</sup> seconds]]. This is the natural time unit proposed by Planck in 1899. It might or might not turn out to be useful to think of it as "the smallest meaningful division of time." One hears speculation about that, but the jury is still out.

The Planck length and Planck time are used as the fundamental units in the system of [[Planck units]].

Max Planck: 'Über irreversible Strahlungsvorgänge'. ''Sitzungsberichte der Preußischen Akademie der Wissenschaften'', vol. 5, p. 479 (1899). 

:''see also:'' [[Electromagnetic radiation]], [[Schrodinger wave equation|Schrödinger's equation]], [[Uncertainty Principle|Heisenberg's uncertainty principle]], [[Wave-Particle duality]]

'''Planck's constant''', denoted ''h'', named after the physicist [[Max Planck]], is a [[physical constants|physical constant]] which appears in all [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanical]] equations. Its value is approximately

:''h'' = 6.6261 × 10<sup>-34</sup> Js 

Planck's constant can be seen as a conversion factor between [[frequency]] and [[energy]], especially for [[photon|photons]]. The [[unicode]] symbol &#8462; (ℎ) can be used for Planck's constant. The abbreviation

:<s>''h''</s> = ''h'' / 2π

where π is [[Pi]], is commonly encountered; the symbol is pronounced "h-bar". The unicode symbol &#8462; (ℏ) can be used for h-bar.

<s>''h''</s> is the quantum of [[angular momentum]]. The angular momentum of any system, measured against any particular choice of axis, is always an integer multiple of this value. <s>''h''</s> also occurs in [[Heisenberg's uncertainty principle|Uncertainy Principle]]. It has therefore been argued that <s>''h''</s> is more fundamental than ''h''. <s>''h''</s> is used to define the [[Planck units]].

''See also:''
[[Electromagnetic radiation]], [[Schrodinger wave equation|Schrödinger's equation]], [[Uncertainty Principle|Heisenberg's uncertainty principle]], [[Wave-Particle duality]]

'''Planck's constant''', denoted ''h'', named after the physicist [[Max Planck]], is a [[physical constants|physical constant]] which appears in all [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanical]] equations. Its value is approximately

:''h'' = 6.6261 × 10<sup>-34</sup> Js 

Planck's constant can be seen as a conversion factor between [[frequency]] and [[energy]], especially for [[photon|photons]]. The [[unicode]] symbol &#8462; (ℎ) can be used for Planck's constant.

The abbreviation

:<s>''h''</s> = ''h'' / (2π)

where π is [[Pi]], is commonly encountered. It is pronounced as "h-bar". The constant <s>''h''</s> is sometimes referred to as '''Dirac's constant''' after [[Paul Dirac]]. The unicode symbol &#8463; (ℏ) can be used for this on some browsers.

<s>''h''</s> is the quantum of [[angular momentum]], including [[spin (physics)|spin]]. The angular momentum of any system, measured against any particular choice of axis, is always an integer multiple of this value. <s>''h''</s> also occurs in [[Uncertainty Principle|Heisenberg's uncertainty principle]]. It has therefore been argued that <s>''h''</s> is more fundamental than ''h''. <s>''h''</s> is used to define the [[Planck units]].

''see also: [[Electromagnetic radiation]], [[Schrodinger equation|Schrödinger equation]], [[Uncertainty Principle|Heisenberg's uncertainty principle]], [[Wave-Particle duality]], [[Quantum Hall effect]]''

'''Planck's constant''', denoted ''h'', named after the physicist [[Max Planck]], is a [[physical constants|physical constant]] which appears in all [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanical]] equations. Its value is approximately

:''h'' = 6.6261 × 10<sup>-34</sup> Js 

Planck's constant can be seen as a conversion factor between [[frequency]] and [[energy]], especially for [[photon]]s. The [[unicode]] symbol &#8462; (ℎ) can be used for Planck's constant.

The abbreviation

:<math>\hbar = \frac{h}{2\pi}</math>

where π is [[Pi]], is commonly encountered. It is pronounced as "h-bar". The constant <math>\hbar</math> is sometimes referred to as '''Dirac's constant''' after [[Paul Dirac]]. The unicode symbol &#8463; (ℏ) can be used for this on some browsers.

<math>\hbar</math> is the quantum of [[angular momentum]], including [[spin (physics)|spin]]. The angular momentum of any system, measured against any particular choice of axis, is always an integer multiple of this value. <math>\hbar</math> also occurs in [[Uncertainty Principle|Heisenberg's uncertainty principle]]. It has therefore been argued that <math>\hbar</math> is more fundamental than ''h''. <math>\hbar</math> is used to define the [[Planck units]].

''see also: [[Electromagnetic radiation]], [[Schrödinger equation]], [[Uncertainty Principle|Heisenberg's uncertainty principle]], [[Wave-particle duality]], [[Quantum Hall effect]]''

[[ca:Constant de Planck]]
[[fr:Constante de Planck]] [[it:Costante di Plank]] [[ja:プランク定数]] [[ko:플랑크상수]] [[nl:Constante van Planck]] [[pl:Sta%C5%82a Plancka]] [[sl:Planckova konstanta]]

'''Planck's constant''', denoted ''h'', is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory. It has a value of approximately

:<math>h = 6.6261 \times 10^{-34} Js. </math>

Planck's constant has [[Physical unit|units]] of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are the units of [[action (physics)|action]]. These units may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]], which are the units of [[angular momentum]].

A closely-related quantity is

:<math>\hbar \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi},</math>

where π is the constant [[pi]]. This constant, which is pronounced as "h-bar", is sometimes (though infrequently) referred to as '''Dirac's constant''', after [[Paul Dirac]]. 

Planck's constant is used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in microscopic [[particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. For instance, the [[energy]] ''E'' carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] ''ν'' can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n=0,1,2,3,...</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] ''ω=2πν'', which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n=0,1,2,3,...</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, ... \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, ..., j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Uncertainty Principle|Heisenberg's uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]] in any position measurement, ''Δx'', and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, ''Δp'', obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p = \frac{1}{2}\hbar</math>

On some browsers, the [[unicode]] symbol &#8462; (ℎ) is rendered as Planck's constant, and the symbol &#8463; (ℏ) is rendered as Dirac's constant.

== See also ==

* [[Electromagnetic radiation]]
* [[Natural units]]
* [[Schrödinger equation]]
* [[Wave-particle duality]]
* [[Quantum Hall effect]]

[[de:Plancksches Wirkungsquantum]]
[[fr:Constante de Planck]] [[it:Costante di Plank]] [[ja:プランク定数]] [[ko:플랑크상수]] [[nl:Constante van Planck]] [[pl:Sta%C5%82a Plancka]] [[sl:Planckova konstanta]]

'''Planck's constant''', denoted ''h'', is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory. It has a value of approximately

:<math>h=6.626 068 76(52)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>. 

Planck's constant has [[Physical unit|units]] of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are the units of [[action (physics)|action]]. These units may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[Newton|N]]<math>\cdot</math>[[metre|m]]<math>\cdot</math>[[second|s]]), which are the units of [[angular momentum]].

A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''':

:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi}=1.054 571 596(82)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s},</math>

where π is the constant [[pi]]. This constant, which is pronounced as "h-bar", is sometimes (though infrequently) referred to as '''Dirac's constant''', after [[Paul Dirac]]. 

Planck's constant is used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in microscopic [[particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. For instance, the [[energy]] ''E'' carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] ''ν'' can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n=0,1,2,3,...</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] ''ω=2πν'', which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n=0,1,2,3,...</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, ... \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, ..., j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]] in any position measurement, ''Δx'', and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, ''Δp'', obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p = \frac{1}{2}\hbar</math>

On some browsers, the [[Unicode]] symbol &#8462; (ℎ) is rendered as Planck's constant, and the symbol &#8463; (ℏ) is rendered as Dirac's constant.

== See also ==

* [[Electromagnetic radiation]]
* [[Natural units]]
* [[Schrödinger equation]]
* [[Wave-particle duality]]
* [[Quantum Hall effect]]

'''Planck's constant''', denoted ''h'', is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory. It has a value of approximately

:<math>h=6.626 068 76(52)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>. 

Planck's constant has [[Physical unit|units]] of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are the units of [[action (physics)|action]]. These units may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[Newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are the units of [[angular momentum]].

A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (sometimes called ''Dirac's constant''):

:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi}=1.054 571 596(82)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s},</math>

where π is the constant [[pi]]. This constant is pronounced as "h-bar".

Planck's constant is used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in microscopic [[particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. For instance, the [[energy]] ''E'' carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] ''ν'' can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n=0,1,2,3,...</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] ''ω=2πν'', which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n=0,1,2,3,...</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, ... \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, ..., j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]] in any position measurement, ''Δx'', and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, ''Δp'', obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p = \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

On some browsers, the [[Unicode]] symbol &#8462; (ℎ) is rendered as Planck's constant, and the symbol &#8463; (ℏ) is rendered as Dirac's constant.

'''Planck's constant''', denoted ''h'', is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory. It has a value of approximately

:<math>h=6.626\ 069\ 3(11)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>. 

Planck's constant has [[Physical unit|units]] of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are the units of [[action (physics)|action]]. These units may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[Newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are the units of [[angular momentum]].

A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (sometimes called ''Dirac's constant''):

:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi}=1.054\ 571\ 68(18)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s},</math>

where π is the constant [[pi]]. This constant is pronounced as "h-bar".

The figures cited here are the 2002 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2002 CODATA results were made available in December 2003 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available through 31 December 2002.  New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

Planck's constant is used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in microscopic [[particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. For instance, the [[energy]] ''E'' carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] ''ν'' can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] ''ω=2πν'', which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, ''Δx'', and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, ''Δp'', obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

On some browsers, the [[Unicode]] symbol &#8462; (ℎ) is rendered as Planck's constant, and the symbol &#8463; (ℏ) is rendered as Dirac's constant.

:<math>h=6.626\ 069\ 3(11)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>. 
With [[electronvolt|eV]]·[[second|s]] as energy unit:
:<math>h=4.135\ 667\ 43(35)\ \times10^{-15} \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2002 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2002 CODATA results were made available in December 2003 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available through 31 December 2002.  New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

'''Planck's constant''', denoted ''h'', is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory. Its value is
:<math>h=6.626\ 069\ 3(11) \times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>
or with [[electronvolt]]s as energy unit:
:<math>h=4.135\ 667\ 43(35) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math>

Planck's constant has [[Physical unit|units]] of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are the units of [[action (physics)|action]]. These units may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[Newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are the units of [[angular momentum]].

A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (sometimes called ''Dirac's constant''):

:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi}=1.054\ 571\ 68(18)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s},</math>

where π is the constant [[pi]]. This constant is pronounced as "h-bar".

The figures cited here are the 2002 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2002 CODATA results were made available in December 2003 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available through 31 December 2002.  New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

Planck's constant is used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in microscopic [[particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. For instance, the [[energy]] ''E'' carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] ''ν'' can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] ''ω'' = 2 π'' ν'', which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, Δ''x'', and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, Δ''p'', obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

On some browsers, the [[Unicode]] symbol &#8462; (ℎ) is rendered as Planck's constant, and the symbol &#8463; (ℏ) is rendered as Dirac's constant.

'''Planck's constant''', denoted ''h'', is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory. Its value is
:<math>h=6.626\ 0693(11) \times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>
or with [[electronvolt]]s as energy unit:
:<math>h=4.135\ 667\ 43(35) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math>
[[Image:MaxPlanckWirkungsquantums20050815 CopyrightKaihsuTai.jpg|thumb|right|A commemoration plaque for Max Planck on his discovery of Planck's constant, in front of Humboldt University, Berlin]]
Planck's constant has [[Physical unit|units]] of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are the units of [[action (physics)|action]]. These units may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[Newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are the units of [[angular momentum]].

A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (sometimes called ''[[Paul Dirac|Dirac]]'s constant''):

:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi}=1.054\ 571\ 68(18)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s},</math>

where π is the constant [[pi]]. This constant is pronounced as "h-bar".

The figures cited here are the 2002 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2002 CODATA results were made available in December 2003 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available through 31 December 2002.  New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

Planck's constant is used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in microscopic [[particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. For instance, the [[energy]] ''E'' carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] ''ν'' can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] ''ω'' = 2 π'' ν'', which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, Δ''x'', and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, Δ''p'', obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

On some browsers, the [[Unicode]] symbol &#8462; (ℎ) is rendered as Planck's constant, and the symbol &#8463; (ℏ) is rendered as Dirac's constant.

'''Planck's constant''' (denoted ''h'') is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory.  A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (denoted <math>\hbar</math>, pronounced as "h-bar"), which is sometimes called '''Dirac's constant''', after [[Paul Dirac]].

Planck's constant and the reduced Planck's constant are used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in microscopic [[particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. 

==Units, value and symbols==
Planck's constant has [[Physical unit|units]] of [[energy]] ([[joule]]s or J) multiplied by [[time]] ([[second]]s or s), which are the units of [[action (physics)|action]] ([[joule second]]s or J·s). These units may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton]]·[[metre]]·[[second]] or N·m·s), which are the units of [[angular momentum]].  

The value of Planck's constant, in joule seconds, is:
:<math>h=6.626\ 0693(11) \times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>
or, with [[electronvolt]]s (eV) as the unit of [[energy]]:
:<math>h=4.135\ 667\ 43(35) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math>

The value of the reduced Planck's constant, in joule seconds, is:
:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi}=1.054\ 571\ 68(18)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s},</math>
where π is the constant [[pi]] (3.141…).

The figures cited here are the 2002 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2002 CODATA results were made available in December 2003 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of [[31 December]] [[2002]].  New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

On some browsers, the [[Unicode]] symbol &#8462; ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) is rendered as Planck's constant, and the symbol &#8463; ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) is rendered as Dirac's constant.


==Origins of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant==

Planck's constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed by [[Max Planck]] in reference to the problem of [[black-body radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a blackbody could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> or [[angular frequency]] of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[radian]]/sec).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption".  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics]].

Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math>2 \pi \ </math>. The [[SI]] unit of measurement of Planck's constant is joule-[[hertz]], or joule per ([[turn]] per second), while the unit of measurement of Dirac's constant is joule per ([[radian]] per second). The two constants are merely conversion factors between energy units and frequency units. Physicists tend to omit the unit names for angle, and so they need two constants for the same concept. 

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
Planck's constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'')carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

Planck's constant has [[Physical unit|units]] of [[energy]] ([[joule]]s or J) multiplied by [[time]] ([[second]]s or s), which are the units of [[acch are the units of [[angular momentum]].  

The value of Planck's constant, in joule seconds, is:
:<math>h=6.626\ 0693(11) \times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>
or, with [[electronvolt]]s (eV) as the unit of [[energy]]:
:<math>h=4.135\ 667\ 43(35) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math>
(The digits in brackets signify the uncertainty ([[standard deviation]]) in the last digits of the value).

The value of the reduced Planck's constant, in joule seconds, is:
:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi}=1.054\ 571\ 68(18)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s},</math>
or, with [[electronvolt]]s (eV) as the unit of [[energy]]:
:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi}=6.582\ 119\ 15(56) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math>
where π is the constant [[pi]] (3.141…).

The figures cited here are the 2002 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2002 CODATA results were made available in December 2003 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of [[31 December]] [[2002]].  New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

On some browsers, the [[Unicode]] symbol  ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) is rendered as Planck's constant, and the symbol  ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) is rendered as Dirac's constant.

==Origins of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant==

Planck's constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> or [[angular frequency]] of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[radian]]/sec).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption".  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics]].

Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math>2 \pi \ </math>. The [[SI]] unit of measurement of Planck's constant is joule per [[hertz]], or joule per ([[turn]] per second), while the unit of measurement of Dirac's constant is joule per ([[radian]] per second). The two constants are merely conversion factors between energy units and frequency units.

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
Planck's constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'')carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

Planck's constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'')carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

[[Image:MaxPlanckWirkungsquantums20050815 CopyrightKaihsuTai.jpg|thumb|right|A commemoration plaque for [[Max Planck]] on his discovery of Planck's constant, in front of [[Humboldt University]], [[Berlin]]. English translation: "Max Planck, discoverer of the elementary quantum of action ''h'', taught in this house from 1889 to 1928."]]

'''Planck's constant''' (denoted ''h'') is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory.  A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (denoted <math>\hbar</math>, pronounced "h-bar"). Planck's constant is also used in measuring energy emitted by light photons, such as in the equation E=h<math>\nu</math>, where E is energy, h is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek letter]] nu) is frequency.

Planck's constant and the reduced Planck's constant are used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in subatomic [[elementary particle|particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. 

==Units, value and symbols==
Planck's constant has [[Physical unit|units]] of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are the units of [[action (physics)|action]] ([[joule second|J·s]]). These units may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the units of [[angular momentum]]. However, often the unit of choice is [[Electron volt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of Planck's constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 0693 (11) \times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 43(35) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the uncertainty ([[standard deviation]]) in the last two digits of the value.

The value of Dirac's constant (also known as the ''reduced Planck's constant'', and called h-dash when you are unable to use the symbol) is:
:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 68(18)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 119\ 15(56) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2002 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2002 CODATA results were made available in December 2003 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of [[31 December]] [[2002]].  New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

On some browsers, the [[Unicode]] symbol  ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) is rendered as Planck's constant, and the symbol  ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) is rendered as Dirac's constant.

==Origins of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant==

Planck's constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon|photons]] of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian]]/sec) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption".  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The [[SI]] unit of measurement of Planck's constant is joule per [[hertz]], or joule per ([[turn]] per second), while the unit of measurement of Dirac's constant is joule per ([[radian]] per second). The two constants are merely conversion factors between energy units and frequency units.

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
Planck's constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The [[SI]] unit of measurement of Planck's constant is joule per [[hertz]], or joule per ([[turn]] per second), while the unit of measurement of Dirac's constant is joule per ([[radian]] per second). The two constants are merely conversion factors between energy units and frequency units.

[[Image:MaxPlanckWirkungsquantums20050815 CopyrightKaihsuTai.jpg|thumb|right|A commemoration plaque for [[Max Planck]] on his discovery of Planck's constant, in front of [[Humboldt University]], [[Berlin]]. English translation: "Max Planck, discoverer of the elementary quantum of action ''h'', taught in this house from 1889 to 1928."]]

'''Planck's constant''' (pronounced like "plank" or "plonk"; denoted ''h'') is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory.  A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''Dirac's constant''' and denoted <math>\hbar</math>, pronounced "h-bar" or "h-cross"). Planck's constant is also used in measuring energy emitted by light photons, such as in the equation E=''h''<math>\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency.

Planck's constant and the reduced Planck's constant are used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in subatomic [[elementary particle|particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. 

==Units, value and symbols==
Planck's constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]] Planck's constant is expressed in [[Joule second|Joule-seconds]]. The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electron volt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of Planck's constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 0693 (11) \times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 43(35) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the uncertainty ([[standard deviation]]) in the last two digits of the value.

The value of Dirac's constant (also known as the ''reduced Planck's constant'', and referred to as "h-bar" when you are unable to use the symbol) is:
:<math>\hbar\equiv\frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 68(18)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 119\ 15(56) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2002 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2002 CODATA results were made available in December 2003 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of [[31 December]] [[2002]].  New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for Planck's constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for Dirac's constant.

==Origins of Planck's constant==

Planck's constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian]]/sec) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption".  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
Planck's constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac's constant==
Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The [[SI]] unit of measurement of Planck's constant is joule per [[hertz]], or joule per ([[turn]] per second), while the unit of measurement of Dirac's constant is joule per ([[radian]] per second). The two constants are merely conversion factors between energy units and frequency units.

The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac's constant==
Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The [[SI]] unit of measurement of Planck's constant is joule per [[hertz]], or joule per ([[turn]] per second), while the unit of measurement of Dirac's constant is joule per ([[radian]] per second). The two constants are merely conversion factors between energy units and frequency units.

[[Image:MaxPlanckWirkungsquantums20050815 CopyrightKaihsuTai.jpg|thumb|right|A commemoration plaque for [[Max Planck]] on his discovery of Planck's constant, in front of [[Humboldt University]], [[Berlin]]. English translation: "Max Planck, discoverer of the elementary quantum of action ''h'', taught in this house from 1889 to 1928."]]

'''Planck's constant''' (denoted ''h'') is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory.  A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''Dirac's constant''' and denoted <math>\hbar</math>, pronounced "h-bar"). Planck's constant is also used in measuring energy emitted by light photons, such as in the equation E=''h''<math>\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency.

Planck's constant and the reduced Planck's constant are used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in subatomic [[elementary particle|particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values. 

The value of Planck's constant is:

:<math>h = 6.626\ 0693 (11) \times10^{-34}\ \mathrm{J}\cdot\mathrm{s}</math>
:<math>= 4.135\ 667\ 43(35) \times10^{-15}\ \mathrm{eV}\cdot\mathrm{s}</math>

==Units, value and symbols==
Planck's constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]] Planck's constant is expressed in [[Joule second|Joule-seconds]]. The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]•[[metre|m]]•[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electron volt|eV]]•s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of Planck's constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.911\ 0693 (11) \times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 43(35) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the uncertainty ([[standard deviation]]) in the last two digits of the value.

The value of Dirac's constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \stackrel{\mathrm{def}}{=}\ \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 68(18)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 119\ 15(56) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2002 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2002 CODATA results were made available in December 2003 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of [[31 December]] [[2002]].  New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for Planck's constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for Dirac's constant.

==Origins of Planck's constant==

Planck's constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian]]/sec) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption".  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
Planck's constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac's constant==
Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. Planck's constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while Dirac's constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]). Both constants are conversion factors between energy units and frequency units.

In essence, Dirac's constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in Joule·seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. All other uses of Plank's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\

==Significance of the Size of Planck's constant==

Expressed in the S.I. units of <math>\mathrm{J}\cdot\mathrm{s}</math>, Planck's constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics.  The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with classical physics.  Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as Planck's constant tends to zero.  However, in the natural units describing physics at the atomic scale, Planck's constant becomes equal to 1, reflecting the fact that physics at the atomic scale is dominated by quantum effects.

Planck's constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac's constant==
Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. Planck's constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while Dirac's constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]). Both constants are conversion factors between energy units and frequency units.

In essence, Dirac's constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

==Significance of the size of Planck's constant==

Expressed in the S.I. units of J·s, Planck's constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics.  The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with classical physics.  Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as Planck's constant tends to zero.  However, in the natural units describing physics at the atomic scale, Planck's constant becomes equal to 1, reflecting the fact that physics at the atomic scale is dominated by quantum effects.

[[Image:MaxPlanckWirkungsquantums20050815 CopyrightKaihsuTai.jpg|thumb|right|A commemoration plaque for [[Max Planck]] on his discovery of Planck's constant, in front of [[Humboldt University]], [[Berlin]]. English translation: "Max Planck, discoverer of the elementary quantum of action ''h'', taught in this building from 1889 to 1928."]]

'''Planck's constant''' (denoted ''h'') is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory.  A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''Dirac's constant''' and denoted ħ, pronounced "h-bar"). Planck's constant is also used in measuring energy emitted by light photons, such as in the equation E=''h''<math>\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency.

Planck's constant and the reduced Planck's constant are used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in subatomic [[elementary particle|particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values.

==Units, value and symbols==
Planck's constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]] Planck's constant is expressed in [[Joule second|joule-seconds]]. The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of Planck's constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 068\ 96(33) \times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the uncertainty ([[standard deviation]]) in the last two digits of the value.

The value of Dirac's constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for Planck's constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for Dirac's constant.

==Origins of Planck's constant==

Planck's constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption".  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
Planck's constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac's constant==
Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. Planck's constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while Dirac's constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]). Both constants are conversion factors between energy units and frequency units.

In essence, Dirac's constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

==Significance of the size of Planck's constant==

Expressed in the S.I. units of J·s, Planck's constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics.  The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with classical physics.  Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as Planck's constant tends to zero.  However, in the natural units describing physics at the atomic scale, Planck's constant becomes equal to 1, reflecting the fact that physics at the atomic scale is dominated by quantum effects.

The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for Planck's constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for Dirac's constant.

==Origins of Planck's constant==

Planck's constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body]] [[radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption".  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
Planck's constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>\begin{matrix}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j\end{matrix}.</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

Planck's constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. The [[uncertainty]]  (more precisely: the [[standard deviation]]) in any position measurement, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in a momentum measurement along the same direction, <math>\Delta p</math>, obeys

:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar.</math>

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac's constant==
Dirac's constant or the "''reduced'' Planck's constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from Planck's constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. Planck's constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while Dirac's constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]). Both constants are conversion factors between energy units and frequency units.

In essence, Dirac's constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

==Significance of the size of Planck's constant==

Expressed in the [[SI units]] of J·s, Planck's constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics.  The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with [[classical physics]].  Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as Planck's constant tends to zero.  However, in the [[natural units]] describing physics at the atomic scale, Planck's constant becomes equal to 1, reflecting the fact that physics at the atomic scale is dominated by quantum effects.

It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory.  A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''Dirac's constant''' and denoted '''''ħ''''', pronounced "h-bar"). The Planck constant is also used in measuring energy emitted as [[photon]]s, such as in the equation E=''h''<math>\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency.

The Planck constant and the reduced Planck constant are used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in subatomic [[elementary particle|particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values.

==Units, value and symbols==
The Planck constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]], the Planck constant is expressed in [[Joule second|joule-seconds]]. The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of the Planck constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 068\ 96(33) \times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the standard uncertainty in the last two digits of the value.

The value of the Dirac constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.
