This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption".  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

where <math>\delta_{ij}</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
The Planck constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''ν'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>
\begin{align}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,\quad  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots,  \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j.
\end{align}

Given a large number of particles prepared in the same state, the [[uncertainty]] in their position, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in their momentum (in the same direction), <math>\Delta p</math>, obey

==Dirac constant==
The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

In essence, the Dirac constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

==Significance of the size of Planck's constant==
Expressed in the [[SI units]] of J·s, the Planck constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics. The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with [[classical physics]]. Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as the Planck constant tends to zero. In [[natural units]] the Dirac constant is taken as 1 (i.e. the Planck constant is 2·π), as is convenient for describing physics at the atomic scale dominated by quantum effects.

[[Image:MaxPlanckWirkungsquantums20050815 CopyrightKaihsuTai.jpg|thumb|right|A commemoration plaque for [[Max Planck]] on his discovery of Planck's constant, in front of [[Humboldt University]], [[Berlin]]. English translation: "Max Planck, discoverer of the elementary quantum of action ''h'', taught in this building from 1889 to 1928."]]

The '''Planck constant''' (denoted '''''<math>h</math>''''') is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory.  A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''Dirac's constant''' and denoted '''''<math>\hbar</math>''''', pronounced "h-bar"). The Planck constant is also used in measuring energy emitted as [[photon]]s, such as in the equation E=''h''<math>\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency.

The Planck constant and the reduced Planck constant are used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in subatomic [[elementary particle|particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values.

==Significance of the size of Planck's constant==
Expressed in the [[SI units]] of [[joule second]]s (J·s), the Planck constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics. The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with [[classical physics]]. Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as the Planck constant tends to zero.

In [[natural units]], the Dirac constant is taken as 1 (''i.e.'', the Planck constant is 2·π), as is convenient for describing physics at the atomic scale dominated by quantum effects.

==Units, value and symbols==
The Planck constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]], the Planck constant is expressed in [[Joule second|joule seconds]] (J·s). The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of the Planck constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 068\ 96(33) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the standard uncertainty in the last two digits of the value.

The value of the Dirac constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for the Planck constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for the Dirac constant.

==Origins of Planck's constant==

The Planck constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body]] [[radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption".  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}\,</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
The Planck constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''<math>\nu\,</math>'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>
\begin{align}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,\quad  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots,  \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j.
\end{align}
</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

The Planck constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. Given a large number of particles prepared in the same state, the [[uncertainty]] in their position, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in their momentum (in the same direction), <math>\Delta p</math>, obey

:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

where the uncertainty is given as the [[standard deviation]] of the measured value from its [[expected value]].

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac constant==
The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

In essence, the Dirac constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. The Planck constant is similarly a conversion factor between phase (in cycles) and action. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

===More recent values===
In October 2007, the National Physical Laboratory reported initial measurements of the Planck constant using a newly improved watt balance.  They report a value of:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 070\ 95(44) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, </math>
which is not consistent with the current CODATA value above (Robinson and Kibble 2007).

The Planck constant is similarly a conversion factor between phase (in cycles) and action. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

[[Image:MaxPlanckWirkungsquantums20050815 CopyrightKaihsuTai.jpg|thumb|right|A commemoration plaque for [[Max Planck]] on his discovery of Planck's constant, in front of [[Humboldt University]], [[Berlin]]. English translation: "Max Planck, discoverer of the elementary quantum of action ''h'', taught in this building from 1889 to 1928."]]

The '''Planck constant''' (denoted '''''<math>h</math>''''') is a [[physical constant]] that is used to describe the sizes of [[quantum|quanta]]. It plays a central role in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory.  A closely-related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''Dirac's constant''' and denoted '''''<math>\hbar</math>''''', pronounced "h-bar"). The Planck constant is also used in measuring energy emitted as [[photon]]s, such as in the equation <math>E=h\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency.

The Planck constant and the reduced Planck constant are used to describe quantization, a phenomenon occurring in subatomic [[elementary particle|particle]]s such as [[electron]]s and [[photon]]s in which certain physical properties occur in fixed amounts rather than assuming a continuous range of possible values.

==Significance of the size of Planck's constant==
Expressed in the [[SI units]] of [[joule second]]s (J·s), the Planck constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics. The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with [[classical physics]]. Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as the Planck constant tends to zero.

In [[natural units]], the Dirac constant is taken as 1 (''i.e.'', the Planck constant is 2·π), as is convenient for describing physics at the atomic scale dominated by quantum effects.

==Units, value and symbols==
The Planck constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]], the Planck constant is expressed in [[Joule second|joule seconds]] (J·s). The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of the Planck constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 068\ 96(33) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the standard uncertainty in the last two digits of the value.

The value of the Dirac constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for the Planck constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for the Dirac constant.

===More recent values===
In October 2007, the National Physical Laboratory reported initial measurements of the Planck constant using a newly improved watt balance.  They report a value of:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 070\ 95(44) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, </math>
which is not consistent with the current CODATA value above (Robinson and Kibble 2007).

==Origins of Planck's constant==

The Planck constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body]] [[radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law|Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption"{{Fact|date=December 2007}}.  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}\,</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
The Planck constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''<math>\nu\,</math>'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>
\begin{align}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,\quad  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots,  \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j.
\end{align}
</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

The Planck constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. Given a large number of particles prepared in the same state, the [[uncertainty]] in their position, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in their momentum (in the same direction), <math>\Delta p</math>, obey

:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

where the uncertainty is given as the [[standard deviation]] of the measured value from its [[expected value]].

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac constant==
The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

In essence, the Dirac constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. The Planck constant is similarly a conversion factor between phase (in cycles) and action. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of the Planck constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.636\ 068\ 96(33) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the standard uncertainty in the last two digits of the value.

The value of the Dirac constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for the Planck constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for the Dirac constant.

===More recent values===
In October 2005, the National Physical Laboratory reported initial measurements of the Planck constant using a newly improved watt balance.  They report a value of:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 070\ 95(44) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, </math>
which is not consistent with the current CODATA value above (Robinson and Kibble 2007).

==Origins of Planck's constant==

The Planck constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body]] [[radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law|Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved extremely accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption"{{Fact|date=December 2007}}.  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}\,</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
The Planck constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''<math>\nu\,</math>'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>
\begin{align}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,\quad  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots,  \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j.
\end{align}
</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

The Planck constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. Given a large number of particles prepared in the same state, the [[uncertainty]] in their position, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in their momentum (in the same direction), <math>\Delta p</math>, obey

:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

where the uncertainty is given as the [[standard deviation]] of the measured value from its [[expected value]].

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac constant==
The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

In essence, the Dirac constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. The Planck constant is similarly a conversion factor between phase (in cycles) and action. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

It plays a central part in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]], and is named after [[Max Planck]], one of the founders of quantum theory.  A closely related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''Dirac's constant''' and denoted '''''<math>\hbar</math>''''', pronounced "h-bar"). The Planck constant is also used in measuring energy emitted as [[photon]]s, such as in the equation <math>E=h\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency.

Expressed in the [[SI units]] of [[joule second]]s (J·s), the Planck constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics. The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with [[classical physics]]. Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as the Planck constant tends to zero.

In [[natural units]], the Dirac constant is taken as 1 (''i.e.'', the Planck constant is 2·π), as is convenient for describing physics at the atomic scale dominated by quantum effects.

The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

In essence, the Dirac constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. The Planck constant is similarly a conversion factor between phase (in cycles) and action. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

[[Image:MaxPlanckWirkungsquantums20050815 CopyrightKaihsuTai.jpg|thumb|right|A commemoration plaque for [[Max Planck]] on his discovery of Planck's constant, in front of [[Humboldt University]], [[Berlin]]. English translation: "Max Planck, discoverer of the elementary quantum of action ''h'', taught in this building from 1889 to 1928."]]

The '''Planck constant''' (denoted '''''<math>h</math>''''') is a [[physical constant]] that  describes the discrete energy sizes called [[quantum|quanta]] in which [[energy]] occurs within matter. It was first deduced by [[Max Planck]] in the [[photons]] of the [[Electromagnetic_radiation#Electromagnetic_spectrum|electromagnetic spectrum]]. It plays a central part in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]].  A closely related quantity is the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''[[Dirac's constant]]''' and denoted '''''<math>\hbar</math>''''', pronounced "h-bar"). It is the proportionality constant in the equation <math>E=h\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency.

While the Planck constant was first thought to apply only to [[photons]], all matter was recognized to have a [[wave–particle duality]], thus quantization applies to massy particles such as electrons and [[nucleons]] based on their [[momentum]] via the equation <math>p = \hbar k</math> where p is the [[momentum]] and k is the [[wavenumber]] of the object. Recall that all matter moves at velocities ''less than'' the [[speed of light]] and that while photons are massless, they nonetheless carry a momentum.

==Significance of the size of Planck's constant==
Expressed in the [[SI units]] of [[joule second]]s (J·s), the Planck constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics. The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with [[classical physics]]. Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as the Planck constant tends to zero.

In [[natural units]], the Dirac constant is taken as 1 (''i.e.'', the Planck constant is 2·π), as is convenient for describing physics at the atomic scale dominated by quantum effects.

==Units, value and symbols==
The Planck constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]], the Planck constant is expressed in [[Joule second|joule seconds]] (J·s). The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of the Planck constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 068\ 96(33) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math><ref name="NIST">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?h |title=Planck constant |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref><ref name="hev">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?hev |title=Planck constant in eV s |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the standard uncertainty in the last two digits of the value.

The value of the Dirac constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for the Planck constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for the Dirac constant.

===Other reported values===
In October 2005, the National Physical Laboratory reported initial measurements of the Planck constant using a newly improved [[watt balance]].  They report a value of:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 070\ 95(44) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, </math>
which is significantly different (statistically) from the current CODATA value above (Robinson and Kibble 2007).

==Origins of Planck's constant==

The Planck constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body]] [[radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law|Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption"{{Fact|date=December 2007}}.  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}\,</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
The Planck constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''<math>\nu\,</math>'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>
\begin{align}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,\quad  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots,  \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j.
\end{align}
</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

The Planck constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. Given a large number of particles prepared in the same state, the [[uncertainty]] in their position, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in their momentum (in the same direction), <math>\Delta p</math>, obey

:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

where the uncertainty is given as the [[standard deviation]] of the measured value from its [[expected value]].

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac constant==
The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

In essence, the Dirac constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. The Planck constant is similarly a conversion factor between phase (in cycles) and action. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

The '''Planck constant''' (denoted '''''<math>h</math>''''') is a [[physical constant]] that  describes the discrete sizes called [[quantum|quanta]] in which [[momentum]] occurs within [[electromagnetic radiation]] and [[matter]]. It plays a central part in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]]. It is often scaled as the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''[[Dirac's constant]]''' and denoted '''''<math>\hbar</math>''''', pronounced "h-bar"). It was first deduced by [[Max Planck]] in the [[photons]] of the [[Electromagnetic_radiation#Electromagnetic_spectrum|electromagnetic spectrum]]. It is the proportionality constant in the equation <math>E=h\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency.

While the Planck constant was at first thought to apply only to [[photons]], all matter was soon recognized to have a [[wave–particle duality]], thus quantization applies to massy particles such as electrons and [[nucleons]] based on their momentum via the [[De_Broglie_hypothesis|de Broglie equation]] <math>p=\hbar k</math> where p is the momentum and k is the [[wavenumber]] of the object.

All matter moves at velocities ''less than'' the [[speed of light]] and while photons are massless, they nonetheless carry momentum, hence the universal nature of the third equation scaled by the Planck constant, the [[uncertainty principle]].

With these three equations, the Planck constant reminds us once again that the concept of the [[conservation of energy]] and the [[Momentum#Conservation of linear momentum|conservation of momentum]] reflect two distinct realities about the [[universe]] in which we exist. Each must be accounted for separately both in high school [[physics]] and in quantum mechanics.

==Significance of the size of Planck's constant==
Expressed in the [[SI units]] of [[joule second]]s (J·s), the Planck constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics. The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with [[classical physics]]. Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as the Planck constant tends to zero.

In [[natural units]], the Dirac constant is taken as 1 (''i.e.'', the Planck constant is 2·π), as is convenient for describing physics at the atomic scale dominated by quantum effects.

==Units, value and symbols==
The Planck constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]], the Planck constant is expressed in [[Joule second|joule seconds]] (J·s). The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of the Planck constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 068\ 96(33) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math><ref name="NIST">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?h |title=Planck constant |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref><ref name="hev">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?hev |title=Planck constant in eV s |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the standard uncertainty in the last two digits of the value.

The value of the Dirac constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for the Planck constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for the Dirac constant.

===Other reported values===
In October 2005, the National Physical Laboratory reported initial measurements of the Planck constant using a newly improved [[watt balance]].  They report a value of:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 070\ 95(44) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, </math>
which is significantly different (statistically) from the current CODATA value above (Robinson and Kibble 2007).

==Origins of Planck's constant==

The Planck constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body]] [[radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law|Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption"{{Fact|date=December 2007}}.  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}\,</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
The Planck constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''<math>\nu\,</math>'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>
\begin{align}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,\quad  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots,  \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j.
\end{align}
</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

The Planck constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. Given a large number of particles prepared in the same state, the [[uncertainty]] in their position, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in their momentum (in the same direction), <math>\Delta p</math>, obey

:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

where the uncertainty is given as the [[standard deviation]] of the measured value from its [[expected value]].

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac constant==
The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

In essence, the Dirac constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. The Planck constant is similarly a conversion factor between phase (in cycles) and action. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

In [[natural units]], the Dirac constant is taken as 1 (''i.e.'', the Planck constant is 2·π), as is convenient for describing physics at the atomic scale dominated by quantum effects.

==Units, value and symbols==
The Planck constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]], the Planck constant is expressed in [[Joule second|joule seconds]] (J·s). The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of the Planck constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 068\ 96(33) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math><ref name="NIST">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?h |title=Planck constant |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref><ref name="hev">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?hev |title=Planck constant in eV s |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the standard uncertainty in the last two digits of the value.

The value of the Dirac constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for the Planck constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for the Dirac constant.

===Other reported values===
In October 2005, the National Physical Laboratory reported initial measurements of the Planck constant using a newly improved [[watt balance]].  They report a value of:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 070\ 95(44) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, </math>
which is significantly different (statistically) from the current CODATA value above (Robinson and Kibble 2007).

==Origins of Planck's constant==

The Planck constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body]] [[radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law|Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption"{{Fact|date=December 2007}}.  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}\,</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Usage==
The Planck constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''<math>\nu\,</math>'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>
\begin{align}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,\quad  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots,  \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j.
\end{align}
</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

The Planck constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. Given a large number of particles prepared in the same state, the [[uncertainty]] in their position, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in their momentum (in the same direction), <math>\Delta p</math>, obey

:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

where the uncertainty is given as the [[standard deviation]] of the measured value from its [[expected value]].

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac constant==
The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

In essence, the Dirac constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. The Planck constant is similarly a conversion factor between phase (in cycles) and action. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

The '''Planck constant''' (denoted '''''<math>h</math>''''') is a [[physical constant]] that describes the smallest unit of [[action (physics)|action]] (see [[quantum]]) of atom-sized [[particles]] can incrementally acquire. It plays a central part in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]]. It is often scaled as the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''[[Dirac's constant]]''' and denoted '''''<math>\hbar</math>''''', pronounced "h-bar"). It was first deduced by [[Max Planck]] from the energy levels of the photons of the [[Electromagnetic_radiation#Electromagnetic_spectrum|electromagnetic spectrum]]. It is the proportionality constant in the equation <math>E=h\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency. This is also the central equation in the [[photoelectric effect]] whose correct description by Einstein won him his Nobel Prize.

While the Planck constant was at first thought to apply only to [[photons]], all matter was soon recognized to have a [[wave–particle duality]] just like photons, thus quantization applies to massy particles such as electrons and [[nucleons]] based on their momentum via the [[De_Broglie_hypothesis|de Broglie equation]] <math>p=\hbar k</math> where p is the momentum and k is the [[wavenumber]] of the object. 

The units of the Planck constant are units of [[action (physics)|action]] which can be viewed as either momentum times [[distance]] or energy times [[time]]. The [[SI unit]] of <math>h\,</math> is joule second per revolution, and the [[SI unit]] of <math>\hbar</math> is joule second per radian. 

==Units, value and symbols==
The Planck constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]], the Planck constant is expressed in [[Joule second|joule seconds]] (J·s). The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of the Planck constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 068\ 96(33) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math><ref name="NIST">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?h |title=Planck constant |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref><ref name="hev">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?hev |title=Planck constant in eV s |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the standard uncertainty in the last two digits of the value.

The value of the Dirac constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for the Planck constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for the Dirac constant.

===Other reported values===
In October 2005, the National Physical Laboratory reported initial measurements of the Planck constant using a newly improved [[watt balance]].  They report a value of:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 070\ 95(44) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, </math>
which is significantly different (statistically) from the current CODATA value above (Robinson and Kibble 2007).

==Origins of Planck's constant==

The Planck constant, <math> h \ </math>, was proposed in reference to the problem of [[black-body]] [[radiation]].  The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law|Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption"{{Fact|date=December 2007}}.  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}\,</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Physical significance of the Planck constant==
All fermions moves at velocities ''less than'' the [[speed of light]] and have an associated wavelength called the [[Compton wavelength]] and while photons are massless, photons nonetheless carry momentum, hence the universal nature of the third equation scaled by the Planck constant, the [[uncertainty principle]]. These three [[equations]],

:<math> E = h \nu = \hbar \omega \ </math>
:<math>p=\hbar k</math>
:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

emphasize that the Planck constant is a quanta of [[action]] not of either energy or momentum. Note that there are [[Uncertainty principle#Other uncertainty principles|other uncertainty principles]] almost all of which use the Planck constant. The units can be viewed as either momentum times [[distance]] or energy times [[time]]. A weak analogy from [[classical mechanics]] might be made using [[force]], where force times distance is energy but force times time is [[impulse]].  Every important equation in quantum mechanics includes the Planck constant.

===Significance of the size of Planck's constant===
Expressed in the [[SI units]] of [[joule second]]s (J·s), the Planck constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics. The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with [[classical physics]]. Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as the Planck constant tends to zero.

In [[natural units]], the Dirac constant is taken as 1 (''i.e.'', the Planck constant is 2·π), as is convenient for describing physics at the atomic scale dominated by quantum effects.

Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

\begin{align}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,\quad  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots,  \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j.
\end{align}
</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

The Planck constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac constant==
The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules-second per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

The underlying assumption to [[Planck's law|Planck's law of black body radiation]] was that the electromagnetic radiation emitted by a black body could be modeled as a set of [[harmonic oscillators]] with quantized energy of the form:

:<math> E = h \nu = h \omega /(2 \pi)  = \hbar \omega \ </math>

<math> E \ </math> is the quantized energy of the [[photon]]s of radiation having [[frequency]] ([[Hertz|Hz]]) of <math> \nu \ </math> ([[Nu (letter)|nu]]) '''or''' [[angular frequency]] ([[radian|rad]]/s) of <math> \omega \ </math> ([[omega]]).

This model proved accurate, but it provided an intellectual stumbling block for theoreticians who did not understand where the quantization of energy arose — Planck himself only considered it "a purely formal assumption"{{Fact|date=December 2007}}.  This line of questioning helped lead to the formation of [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanics.]]

In addition to some assumptions underlying the interpretation of certain values in the quantum mechanical formulation, one of the fundamental corner-stones to the entire theory lies in the [[commutator]] relationship between the position operator <math>\hat{x}</math> and the momentum operator <math>\hat{p}</math>:

:<math>[\hat{p_i}, \hat{x_j}] = -i \hbar \delta_{ij}</math>

where <math>\delta_{ij}\,</math> is the [[Kronecker delta]].  For more information, see the [[mathematical formulation of quantum mechanics]].

==Physical significance of the Planck constant==
All matter moves at velocities ''less than'' the [[speed of light]] and have an associated wavelength called the [[Compton wavelength]] and while photons are massless, photons nonetheless carry momentum, hence the universal nature of the third equation scaled by the Planck constant, the [[uncertainty principle]]. These three [[equations]],

:<math> E = h \nu = \hbar \omega \ </math>
:<math>p=\hbar k</math>
:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math> or <math>\Delta E \Delta t \ge \hbar </math>

emphasize that the Planck constant is a quanta of [[action]] (i.e. its units are neither of energy nor momentum). Note that there are [[Uncertainty principle#Other uncertainty principles|other uncertainty principles]] almost all of which use the Planck constant. See also the [[Energy#Law of conservation of energy|law of conservation of energy]]. The units can be viewed as either momentum times distance or energy times time. A weak analogy from [[classical mechanics]] might be made using [[force]], where force times distance is energy and force times time is [[impulse]].  Every important equation in quantum mechanics includes the Planck constant either explicitly or implicitly.

Quanta are related not directly to the [[fermions]] of the [[Standard Model]] but to [[hadrons]] and photons (and other bosons). This distinction must be made because [[quarks]] are not directly related to the Planck constant.

===Significance of the size of Planck's constant===
Expressed in the [[SI units]] of [[joule second]]s (J·s), the Planck constant is one of the smallest constants used in physics. The significance of this is that it reflects the extremely small scales at which quantum mechanical effects are observed, and hence why we are not familiar with quantum physics in our everyday lives in the way that we are with [[classical physics]]. Indeed, classical physics can essentially be defined as the limit of quantum mechanics as the Planck constant tends to zero.

In [[natural units]], the Dirac constant is taken as 1 (''i.e.'', the Planck constant is 2·π), as is convenient for describing physics at the atomic scale dominated by quantum effects.

==Usage==
The Planck constant is used to describe quantization. For instance, the [[energy]] (''E'') carried by a beam of [[light]] with constant [[frequency]] (''<math>\nu\,</math>'') can only take on the values

:<math>E = n h \nu \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

It is sometimes more convenient to use the [[angular frequency]] <math>\omega=2\pi\,\nu</math>, which gives

:<math>E = n \hbar \omega \,,\quad n\in\mathbb{N}.</math>

Many such "quantization conditions" exist. A particularly interesting condition governs the quantization of [[angular momentum]]. Let ''J'' be the total angular momentum of a system with rotational invariance, and ''J<sub>z</sub>'' the angular momentum measured along any given direction. These quantities can only take on the values

:<math>
\begin{align}
J^2 = j(j+1) \hbar^2,\quad  & j = 0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, \ldots,  \\
J_z = m \hbar, \qquad\quad\quad & m = -j, -j+1, \ldots, j.
\end{align}
</math>

Thus, <math>\hbar</math> may be said to be the "quantum of angular momentum".

The Planck constant also occurs in statements of [[Werner Heisenberg|Heisenberg's]] [[uncertainty principle]]. Given a large number of particles prepared in the same state, the [[uncertainty]] in their position, <math>\Delta x</math>, and the uncertainty in their momentum (in the same direction), <math>\Delta p</math>, obey

:<math> \Delta x\, \Delta p \ge \begin{matrix}\frac{1}{2}\end{matrix} \hbar</math>

where the uncertainty is given as the [[standard deviation]] of the measured value from its [[expected value]].

There are a number of other such pairs of physically measurable values which obey a similar rule.

==Dirac constant==
The Dirac constant or the "''reduced'' Planck constant", <math> \hbar = \frac{h}{2 \pi} \ </math>, differs only from the Planck constant by a factor of <math> 2 \pi </math>. The Planck constant is stated in [[SI]] units of measurement, joules-second per [[hertz]], or joules per ([[cycle per second]]), while the Dirac constant is the same value stated in joules per ([[radian per second]]).

In essence, the Dirac constant is a conversion factor between [[phase (waves)|phase]] (in radians) and [[action (physics)|action]] (in joule-seconds) as seen in the [[Schrödinger equation]]. The Planck constant is similarly a conversion factor between phase (in cycles) and action. All other uses of Planck's constant and Dirac's constant follow from that.

{{Quantum mechanics}}

The '''Planck constant''' (denoted '''''<math>h</math>''''') is a [[physical constant]] that describes the smallest unit of [[action (physics)|action]] (see [[quantum]]) of atom-sized [[particles]] can incrementally acquire. It plays a central part in the theory of [[quantum mechanics]]. It is often scaled as the '''reduced Planck constant''' (also known as '''[[Dirac's constant]]''' and denoted '''''<math>\hbar</math>''''', pronounced "h-bar"). It was first deduced by [[Max Planck]] from the energy levels of the photons of the [[Electromagnetic_radiation#Electromagnetic_spectrum|electromagnetic spectrum]]. It is the proportionality constant in the equation <math>E=h\nu</math>, where E is energy, ''h'' is Planck's constant, and <math>\nu</math> ([[Greek alphabet|Greek letter]] [[nu_(letter)|nu]]) is frequency. This is also the central equation in the [[photoelectric effect]] whose correct description by Einstein won him his Nobel Prize.

While the Planck constant was at first thought to apply only to [[photons]], all matter was soon recognized to have a [[wave–particle duality]] just like photons, thus quantization applies to massy particles such as electrons and [[nucleons]] based on their momentum via the [[De_Broglie_hypothesis|de Broglie equation]] <math>p=\hbar k</math> where p is the momentum and k is the [[wavenumber]] of the object. It also appears in the [[uncertainty principle]].

The units of the Planck constant are units of [[action (physics)|action]] which can be viewed as either momentum times [[distance]] (per revolution or "cycle") or energy times [[time]] (per revolution). The [[SI unit]] of <math>h\,</math> is joule-second per revolution (or joule per [[hertz]]), and the [[SI unit]] of <math>\hbar</math> is joule second per radian. The "angle" or revolution part of the units is often omitted when the value of the Planck constant is presented.

==Units, value and symbols==
The Planck constant has dimensions of [[energy]] multiplied by [[time]], which are also the dimensions of [[action (physics)|action]]. In [[International System of Units|SI units]], the Planck constant is expressed in [[Joule second|joule seconds]] (J·s). The dimensions may also be written as [[momentum]] times [[distance]] ([[newton|N]]·[[metre|m]]·[[second|s]]), which are also the dimensions of [[angular momentum]]. Often the unit of choice is [[Electronvolt|eV]]·s, because of the small energies that are often encountered in quantum physics.

The value of the Planck constant is:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 068\ 96(33) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\,  4.135\ 667\ 33(10) \times10^{-15}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}.</math><ref name="NIST">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?h |title=Planck constant |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref><ref name="hev">{{cite web |url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?hev |title=Planck constant in eV s |accessdate=2007-08-08 |work=2006 [[CODATA]] recommended values |publisher=[[NIST]] }}</ref>

The two digits between the [[Bracket|parentheses]] denote the standard uncertainty in the last two digits of the value.

The value of the Dirac constant is:
:<math>\hbar\ \equiv \frac{h}{2\pi} = \,\,\, 1.054\ 571\ 628(53)\times10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, = \,\,\, 6.582\ 118\ 99(16) \times10^{-16}\ \mbox{eV}\cdot\mbox{s}</math>

The figures cited here are the 2006 [[CODATA]]-recommended values for the constants and their uncertainties.  The 2006 CODATA results were made available in March 2007 and represent the best-known, internationally-accepted values for these constants, based on all data available as of 31 December 2006. New CODATA figures are scheduled to be published approximately every four years.

[[Unicode]] reserves codepoints U+210E ({{Unicode|ℎ}}) for the Planck constant, and U+210F ({{Unicode|ℏ}}) for the Dirac constant.

===Other reported values===
In October 2005, the National Physical Laboratory reported initial measurements of the Planck constant using a newly improved [[watt balance]].  They report a value of:
:<math>h =\,\,\, 6.626\ 070\ 95(44) \times 10^{-34}\ \mbox{J}\cdot\mbox{s} \,\,\, </math>
which is significantly different (statistically) from the current CODATA value above (Robinson and Kibble 2007).

[[Image:MaxPlanckWirkungsquantums20050815 CopyrightKaihsuTai.jpg|thumb|right|A commemoration plaque for [[Max Planck]] on his discovery of Planck's constant, in front of [[Humboldt University]], [[Berlin]].
